如果政府允许您只燃烧每罐汽油的25％，该怎么办？或者，如果华盛顿让您将每加仑牛奶的一半倒入下水道？
  华盛顿在安全，负担得起的无二氧化碳核能方面正是这样做的。实际上，来自美国104座反应堆的95％的使用过的燃料可被回收再利用，以供美国使用。美国的104座反应堆提供了约20％的电力。
 为了发电，反应堆燃料必须包含3-5％的可燃铀。一旦可燃铀降至该水平以下，则必须更换燃料。但是这个用完了燃料通常保留其最初使用的铀的约95％，并且铀可以循环利用。
 在过去的四十年中，美国的反应堆生产了约56,000吨的废燃料。那是浪费包含足够的能量来为每个美国家庭供电12年。然后就坐在那里，堆放在发电厂的存储设施中。谈论废物！
 可悲的是，美国几十年前就开发了夺回能源的技术，然后在1977年禁止了其商业用途。此后，我们实行了实际上的暂停。
 其他国家没有对核力量采取这种落后的做法。法国有59个反应堆，其发电量占其总电量的80％，几十年来一直安全地回收核燃料。他们在1970年代转向核电以限制对外国能源的依赖。并且，从一开始，他们就将废旧燃料作为其计划的核心。
 从法国的反应堆中取出后，用过的燃料将被包装在集装箱中，并通过火车和公路安全地运到拉海牙的一家设施。在那里，产生铀和p的能源被清除，并与其他废物分离，制成新的燃料，可以再次使用。整个过程增加了法国人6％的成本。 
反核恐惧散布已被证明是毫无根据的。法国已经像这样循环使用了30年的燃料，没有发生任何事件：没有发生恐怖袭击，没有坏蛋偷铀，没有对核武器扩散作出贡献以及发生了意外爆炸。
 法国只需一家工厂即可满足其所有回收需求。事实上，法国国内的后处理仅占拉海格容量的一半。另一半用于回收其他国家/地区。乏核燃料。
 自从开始运营以来，法国的拉海牙工厂已经安全地处理了23,000吨以上的乏燃料，足以为法国供电14年。
 他们的成功在国外引起了很多兴趣。法国公司阿海珐集团已经帮助日本提供了后处理设施，目前正在考虑在中国建立类似工厂的可行性。
  当然，仍然存在浪费。但是回收产生的高放射性废物量要少得多，法国有效地加以处理-将一些废物放在短期，中间存储中，或者将其余的废物准备在Yucca Mountain改建中进行长期存储。
 在法国，一切都不完美。他们仍在努力建立永久性的地质存储设施。但是关键的问题是，他们有一个处理用过的核燃料的组织，可以使他们的计划前进而不会被地质存储的政治束缚。
 如果美国认真对待减少二氧化碳和能源依赖，那么它必须认真对待核电并开始回收废旧核燃料。 
可行的后处理能力不仅会给美国带来宝贵的能源，而且会减少去往丝兰山的材料量。 美国已经产生了足够的废物，几乎可以满足尤卡法律规定的70,000公吨的限制-随后的研究估计其实际产能约为该数量的两倍，有些人认为甚至更大。  这也将使美国重新成为商业核技术的领导者，而今天却不是。  核燃料后处理是一项安全活动，应成为美国核能计划的一部分。 它可以负担得起并且在技术上可行。 法国人每天都在证明这一点。 问题是：为什么不能？
note_en:
What if the government allowed you to burn only 25 percent ofevery tank of gas? Or if Washington made you pour half of everygallon of milk down the drain?
  That is exactly what Washington does when it comes to safe,affordable and CO2-free nuclear energy. Indeed, 95 percent of theused fuel from America's 104 power reactors, which provide about 20percent of the nation's electricity, could be recycled for futureuse.
 To create power, reactor fuel must contain 3-5 percent burnableuranium. Once the burnable uranium falls below that level, the fuelmust be replaced. But this "spent" fuel generally retains about 95percent of the uranium it started with, and that uranium can berecycled.
 Over the past four decades, America's reactors have producedabout 56,000 tons of used fuel. That "waste" contains roughlyenough energy to power every U.S. household for 12 years. And it'sjust sitting there, piling up at power plant storage facilities.Talk about waste!
 The sad thing is, the United States developed the technology torecapture that energy decades ago, then barred its commercial usein 1977. We have practiced a virtual moratorium ever since.
 Other countries have not taken such a backward approach tonuclear power. France, whose 59 reactors generate 80 percent of itselectricity, has safely recycled nuclear fuel for decades. Theyturned to nuclear power in the 1970s to limit their dependence onforeign energy. And, from the beginning, they made recycling usedfuel central to their program.
 Upon its removal from French reactors, used fuel is packed incontainers and safely shipped via train and road to a facility inLa Hague. There, the energy producing uranium and plutonium areremoved and separated from the other waste and made into new fuelthat can be used again. The entire process adds about 6 percent incosts for the French.
 Anti-nuclear fear mongering has proved baseless. The French haverecycled fuel like this for 30 years without incident: no terroristattack, no bad guys stealing uranium, no contribution towardnuclear weapons proliferaton, and o accidental explosions.
 France meets all of its recycling needs with one facility.Indeed, domestic French reprocessing only takes about half of LaHague's capacity. The other half is used to recycle othercountries' spent nuclear fuel.
 Since beginning operations, France's La Hague plant has safelyprocessed over 23,000 tones of used fuel--enough to power Francefor fourteen years.
 Their success has sparked plenty of interest abroad. The Frenchcompany AREVA has already helped Japan with its reprocessingfacility and is currently looking at the feasibility of building asimilar plant in China.
  Of course, there is still waste involved. But recycling producesmuch lower volumes of highly radioactive waste, and the French dealwith it effectively--placing some waste in short-term, interimstorage or preparing the rest for long-term storage in theirversion of Yucca Mountain.
 All is not perfect in France. They are still working to open apermanent geologic storage facility. But the critical issue is thatthey have an organization to handle used nuclear fuel that allowstheir program to advance without being held hostage to the politicsof geologic storage.
 If the United States is serious about reducing CO2 and energydependence, it must get serious about nuclear power and beginrecycling used nuclear fuel.
 A viable reprocessing capability not only would give the UnitedStates a valuable energy resource, it would reduce the amount ofmaterial going to Yucca Mountain. The U.S. has already producedenough waste to nearly fill Yucca's legal limit of 70,000 metrictons--subsequent studies estimate that its actual capacity is aboutdouble that amount and some believe that it is even greater.
 It would also put the United States back on the map as a leaderin commercial nuclear technology, which today it is not.
 Nuclear fuel reprocessing is a safe activity that should be partof America's nuclear energy program. It can be affordable and istechnologically feasible. The French are proving that on a dailybasis. The question is: Why can't oui?
title: 回收核燃料：法国人这样做，为什么不可以？ （2007年）
title_en: Recycling Nuclear Fuel: The French Do It, Why Can't Oui? (2007)

 他补充说，如果该计划获得成功，大约四分之一的财富将流向纽波特市，大约一半将流向为此次检索提供资金的投资者，其余的将归他所有。
   但是，纽波特市议会发言人在给CNN的声明中说，不允许该市发掘该遗址。
 市政厅在很多场合告诉霍威尔斯先生，根据我们的许可许可证，不可能进行挖掘，挖掘本身会对周围地区造成巨大的环境影响，＆＃34;她说。

In 2013, a British IT worker named James Howells threw away a hard drive with a digital wallet containing what was then a little-known, virtually worthless cryptocurrency known as Bitcoin.
 Fast-forward, and at the time of writing a single bitcoin is trading at about $37,000. That means the 7,500 bitcoins contained on the hard drive would be worth in the region of $275 million.
 Now, the Newport, Wales, local has offered his city council a vast sum of money if it allows him to excavate a landfill site where he believes the hard drive has been disposed of.
 "I offered to donate 25% or £52.5 million" — $71.7 million — "to the city of Newport in order to distribute to all local residents who live in Newport should I find and recover the bitcoins,"  Howells told CNN.
   Citing a population for the greater Newport area of about 300,000, Howells said that would mean about £175, or $240, a person.
 "Unfortunately," he said, "they refused the offer and won't even have a face-to-face discussion with me on the matter."
  Howells had mined the bitcoins over four years when cryptocurrencies were still in their infancy and worth very little. Howells recalled throwing the hard drive away between June and August 2013, believing he'd already backed up the files he needed from it.
 He first realized his mistake,  he told BBC News, when the price of a single bitcoin spiked to $1,000 from $150 that same year.
   After visiting the landfill, Howells told the BBC he thought he had "no chance" of retrieving his hard drive. But he now has a new plan to find it.
 "The plan would be to dig a specific area of the landfill based on a grid reference system and recover the hard drive whilst adhering to all safety and environmental standards," Howells told CNN on Friday.
 "The drive would then be presented to data-recovery specialists who can rebuild the drive from scratch with new parts and attempt to recover the tiny piece of data that I need in order to access the bitcoins."
 He added that if the plan were successful, about a quarter of the fortune would go to the city of Newport, roughly half would go to investors who funded the retrieval, and the rest would go to him.
   In a statement to CNN, however, a Newport City Council spokeswoman said the city was not allowed to excavate the site.
 "The council has told Mr. Howells on a number of occasions that excavation is not possible under our licensing permit and excavation itself would have a huge environmental impact on the surrounding area," she said.
 "The cost of digging up the landfill, storing and treating the waste could run into millions of pounds — without any guarantee of either finding it or it still being in working order."
 Howells is, at least,  not alone in his misfortune:  The New York Times reported on Tuesday that bitcoins in lost wallets accounted for about 20% of the existing 18.5 million bitcoins — worth a total of $140 billion.
   Wallet Recovery Services, a firm that helps recover lost digital keys, told The Times that it received 70 requests a day from users trying to access their digital wallets — a number that is three times what it was a month ago.

note:
甚至在赢得白宫之前，拜登（Joe Biden）就对硅谷的批评一直保持不懈，实际上在6月恳求Facebook阻止特朗普总统发表“野蛮主张”。
  拜登（Biden）竞选活动在一封公开信中说：“少了一点，将使Facebook成为误导我们民主的工具。”
  七个月后，暴动分子降落在美国国会大厦，席卷了众议院和参议院，并试图推翻拜登的胜利-进行了致命的，失败的起义，这表明了特朗普虚假的网上信条的腐蚀力。
  这次袭击的后果现在为华盛顿和硅谷之间的政治冲突奠定了基础，因为对Facebook，Google，Twitter及其数字同行的长期挫败感威胁着对历史上的科技行业发起最积极的监管攻击。在就职典礼前夕，拜登和国会民主党领导人已承诺瞄准该国最大的社交媒体平台，因为他们担心这会损害美国民主的结构，以及每天使用这些数字服务的数十亿人。
    “拜登政府知道我们需要更新21世纪的技术法规，”参议员爱德华·J·马基（D-Mass。）说，他长期呼吁进行广泛的改革。
    多年来，民主党领导人提出了一系列新法案，以缩减硅谷的企业规模，限制其对数据的无限需求并阻止网上虚假信息的传播。但是，党的进行调节通话已经长大的日子里更加迫切，因为拜登当选总统，他的党采取了众议院和参议院，特朗普和他的盟友的控制进一步暴露在很大程度上不受监管网络的风险。
  面对过去的审查，Facebook，谷歌和其他硅谷巨头在政治上已经蓬勃发展。根据响应政治中心（Center for Responsive Politics）对联邦最新披露的一项分析，互联网公司在2020年的前9个月中总共花费了超过5900万美元用于游说。现在，许多人正准备在华盛顿展开另一场新的战斗，因为他们试图避免国会和白宫的新努力，这可能会严重限制他们的经商方式。在特朗普任职期间，共和党游说者增加了人手后，包括Facebook在内的一些人争先恐后地雇用更多民主党人。 
“我认为，尤其是对于互联网行业，至少在接下来的两年内将很难做到这一点，”信息技术与创新基金会（Information Technology and Innovation Foundation）总裁罗伯·阿特金森（Rob Atkinson）预测，该智囊团对包括Google和Microsoft在内的公司进行计数在其板上。
      围绕硅谷的监管围困反映出民主党人对该行业的深深挫败，该党认为自2016年总统大选以来，该行业并未因许多错误而弥补。在那场比赛中，随着克里姆林宫在社交媒体网站上散布虚假信息，试图撒下不和并将特朗普送往白宫，Facebook，Google和Twitter成为俄罗斯政府的特工。而且，它暴露了整个科技行业的主要隐私风险，尤其是在Facebook上，由于处理数百万用户的个人信息而不得不为此付出创纪录的50亿美元罚款。
    一波丑闻和失误的浪潮在华盛顿对苹果，亚马逊，Facebook，谷歌和推特进行了一次难以置信的审查，其高管被召集到国会大厦，以在特朗普任职四年期间反复作证其商业行为。然而，在许多有关反托拉斯，隐私和选举安全的辩论中，特朗普扮演的角色微不足道，而是选择奉行旨在惩罚硅谷的政策，以惩罚未经证实的在线审查保守派的指控。
    作为回应，Facebook及其同业做出了重大改变，包括对人工智能进行新的投资，并雇用了数千名工人来监管其滥用平台。 Facebook还成立了整个内部团队，以回应政府日益增长的反竞争行为的信念。
  航空业的努力最终帮助使2020年总统大选免受外国的重大干扰，但这并非没有意外-特朗普及其在线代理人采取了相同的策略来煽动分歧并散布俄罗斯挑衅者四年前如此部署的虚假言论。例如，在Facebook上，特朗普竞选活动及其盟友反复购买了广告，这些广告散布了关于拜登儿子的阴谋论。
  激怒了，拜登和他的竞选活动花了几个月时间恳求Facebook严厉打击。民主党人多次抨击该公司，原因是该公司允许共和党人在线散布虚假信息，而无需将广告提交事实核查。该公司调整了其广告系统后，也实不遵循所有的民主党的诉求，让拜登承诺，如果他赢得大选采取的监管目标的高科技产业。
      有了这一胜利，拜登和他的助手们努力为能够实现其过去承诺的政府奠定早期基础。该小组已表示有兴趣重新考虑第230节，长期以来一直被认为是硅谷的法律圣杯。已有数十年历史的法律免除了对用户和用户发布的内容承担责任的各种站点和服务，包括报纸和社交网络。当选总统和他的盟友，然而，争辩说，法律也基本上已经允许大型科技公司的裙子责任为自己的行为。 
拜登曾在竞选活动中呼吁国会全面废除该法律。甚至不是一个月后他当选，拜登的顶尖新的顾问之一，在信号在乔治敦法一个事件，新政府并未因此放弃其计划。白宫即将上任的副幕僚长布鲁斯·里德（Bruce Reed）公开强调，“让社交媒体公司对其网站和服务的发布行为负责已经很久了”。
    共同提出修订第230条的法案的参议员Richard Blumenthal（D-Conn。）说，他已与拜登过渡小组“广泛地”谈论过重新审视规则并采取“其他步骤，对这些大型企业施加更大的责任”科技公司。”在特朗普病毒式的网上虚假行径的暴徒袭击了美国国会大厦，导致一名警察和四名暴徒死亡的几周前，这些谈话就已经发生了。布卢门撒尔说，这一悲剧可能进一步鼓舞白宫和国会采取行动。
  他说：“我认为新一届国会和新政府将非常重视改革。”
    尤其是Facebook，已经表明它愿意接受监管：其首席执行官马克·扎克伯格（Mark Zuckerberg）去年告诉立法者，他甚至支持对第230节的修改，尽管熟悉该公司思想的人士表示，他们承认国会可能会受到阻碍。规范政治和仇恨言论的复杂性。但是，这家科技巨头在民主党主导的华盛顿仍然面临着巨大的挑战，这一政治现实可能促使该公司在本月初采取了一次不可思议的纪律处分，以禁止特朗普。
  众议员普拉米拉·贾亚帕尔（D-Wash。）表示：“我认为我们正在研究这里的所有选择。”他指责Facebook和其他人“在下届政府即将到来的两周前”采取“机会主义”措施在线惩罚特朗普。在。”
    “我认为有很多迹象表明，我们，民主党人和共和党人以及下一届政府将非常重视社交媒体公司不受管制的力量，”她补充说。
  “有一段时间，科技行业把民主党人视为理所当然，”前Facebook，Twitter和Google通讯官员Nu Wexler说。 “现在，他们面临着新的政治现实。科技仍将处于十字准线。但这将用于更实质性的事情，例如隐私，数据收集和竞争。” 
过去几年，Facebook努力雇用和提升其华盛顿办事处的保守派人士，以试图赢得特朗普政府的青睐。直到本月，即民主党重新获得国会控制权的第二天，该公司才很少批准特朗普，这是试图安抚保守派的战略的一部分。知情人士说，现在，Facebook正在改变路线并重新评估其战略，他们在匿名的情况下发言，因为他们无权公开讨论此事。在国会暴动之后，Facebook是冻结对共和党人政治捐赠的首批主要公司之一。两位知情人士说，首席运营官雪莉•桑德伯格（Sheryl Sandberg）可能会再次扮演更大的角色，就像她在奥巴马执政期间所做的那样。拜登还与Facebook全球事务和通讯副总裁尼克·克莱格（Nick Clegg）建立了长期的私人关系，他曾在奥巴马任职期间担任英国副总理。克莱格基本上就是拜登的对手，而拜登曾经出现在克莱格的播客中。
  两位知情人士说，尽管拜登团队与大型技术有联系，但推广范围却相当有限，尤其是在与公司共享具体计划时。相比之下，特朗普在就职典礼前邀请科技高管赴纽约开会。巴拉克·奥巴马（Barack Obama）总统在他执政期间也进行了早期宣传。
  但总的预期是，拜登将选拔在追求技术方面更具进取心的人。
  即将上任的拜登政府已经将硅谷最有效的批评家列入其行列。公民与人权领导会议主席Vanita Gupta和法律下的公民权利律师委员会主席Kristen Clarke在挑战Facebook，Google和Twitter方面发挥了重要作用-并准备在司法部。在担任现任职务期间，古普塔和克拉克成功地向社交媒体公司施加了压力，要求他们删除有害，虚假的内容，其中包括阻止人们投票的帖子，照片和视频。他们还提出具有里程碑意义的法律案件，指控算法上存在歧视和偏见。
      一位民权律师彼得·罗默·弗里德曼（Peter Romer-Friedman）起诉了Facebook，并与古普塔（Gupta）和克拉克（Clarke）的组织合作。他说：“这些员工充分表明拜登政府将认真对待技术。” “过去四年表明民主党领导人对技术非常怀疑，这与奥巴马政府大不相同。”
      即将上任的政府尚未决定是否选择其他负责监管技术行业的高级政府职位。这包括在美国顶级竞争执法机构开设职位，这是政府起诉Facebook和谷歌（指责它们违反联邦反托拉斯法）时的一系列关键选择。拜登政府准备继承这些案件时，民主党人表示，他们鼓励新任政府选拔严厉的监管机构，以扭转民主党和共和党政府过去的放松管制趋势。
  “我预计政府将对反托拉斯法进行有力的执行，”众议员罗·卡纳（Ro Khanna）（加利福尼亚州）说，他的湾区地区包括苹果等科技公司。 
在国会山，许多国会民主党人承诺要推进他们的长期计划，以使硅谷紧随其后，因为他们将在白宫拥有一个支持性盟友。该党在参议院的一票表决权可能微不足道，但他们认为他们对Facebook，Google和Twitter的挫败感可能是两党的。
  俄勒冈州参议员罗恩·怀登（Ron Wyden）承诺推进有关人工智能的新法规，以确保强大的幕后算法不会因种族或性别而产生偏见。参议员布莱恩·沙茨（Brian Schatz）（夏威夷）表示，他计划尝试恢复其处理在线隐私的法案。以技术为重点的房屋能源和商业委员会的负责人众议员Frank Pallone Jr.（N.J.）表示，他打算让“在线平台对威胁到我们共和国的虚假信息和虚假信息负责。”他说，这包括一项调查，该调查将涉及社交媒体网站在引发国会暴动中扮演的角色。
  沙茨说：“我们实际上可以在这些问题上开展工作，而不是将它们用作相互讨论的话题。” “从技术政策的角度来看，过去四年的一线希望是，有许多周到的工作。 ……我们不乏好主意。”
note_en:
Even before he won the White House, Joe Biden had been unsparing in his criticism of Silicon Valley, practically pleading with Facebook in June to stop President Trump from publishing “wild claims.”
  “Anything less,” the Biden campaign  said in an open letter, “will render Facebook a tool of misinformation that corrodes our democracy.”
  Seven months later, rioters descended on the U.S. Capitol, stormed the House and the Senate, and sought to overturn Biden’s victory — mounting a deadly, failed insurrection that illustrated the corrosive power of Trump’s false online screeds.
  The aftermath of that attack now sets the stage for a political reckoning between Washington and Silicon Valley, as long-simmering frustrations with Facebook, Google, Twitter and their digital peers threaten to unleash the  most aggressive regulatory assault against the tech industry in its history. On the eve of his inauguration, Biden and Democratic leaders in Congress are pledging to take aim at the country’s largest social media platforms out of concern that they imperil the very fabric of American democracy — and the billions of people who use these digital services every day.
    “The Biden administration knows we need to update our technology regulations for the 21st century,” said Sen. Edward J. Markey (D-Mass.), who has long called for wide-ranging reform.
    Democratic leaders for years have proposed a bevy of new legislation to shrink Silicon Valley’s corporate footprint, restrict its insatiable appetite for data and stop the spread of falsehoods online. But the party’s calls for regulation have grown more urgent in the days since Biden won the presidency, his party took control of the House and the Senate, and Trump and his allies further exposed the risks of a largely unregulated Web.
  In the face of past scrutiny, Facebook, Google and other Silicon Valley giants have muscled up politically. Internet companies together spent  more than $59 million in lobbying over the first nine months of 2020, according to an analysis of the most recent federal disclosures by the Center for Responsive Politics. Many are now bracing for another fresh battle in Washington, as they seek to stave off new efforts by Congress and the White House that might severely restrict the way they do business. Some, including Facebook, are scrambling to hire more Democrats after staffing up on Republican lobbyists during the Trump years.
    “I think for the Internet industry, in particular, it’s going to be tough sledding for the next two years at least,” predicted Rob Atkinson, the president of the Information Technology and Innovation Foundation, a think tank that counts companies including Google and Microsoft on its board.
      The regulatory siege looming over Silicon Valley reflects Democrats’ deep-seated frustration with an industry that the party thinks has not atoned for its many mistakes since the 2016 presidential election. In that race, Facebook, Google and Twitter  were turned into agents of the Russian government, as the Kremlin spread disinformation on social media sites in an attempt to sow discord and send Trump to the White House. And it exposed major privacy risks across the tech sector, especially at Facebook, which had to pay a  record-setting $5 billion penalty for mishandling millions of users’ personal information.
    The wave of scandals and missteps resulted in once-unfathomable scrutiny in Washington for Apple, Amazon, Facebook, Google and Twitter, whose executives were  called to the Capitol to testify about their business practices repeatedly during Trump’s four years in office. Trump, however, played a minimal role in many of those debates about antitrust, privacy and election security, choosing instead to pursue policies that aimed to punish Silicon Valley over unproven  allegations that it censored conservatives online.
    Facebook and its peers responded by instituting significant changes to their businesses, including new efforts to invest in artificial intelligence and hire thousands of workers to police their platforms for abuse. Facebook also created entire internal teams to respond to the government’s growing belief that it had acted anti-competitively.
  The industry’s efforts ultimately helped shield the 2020 presidential race from major  foreign interference, but it was not without incident — as Trump and his online surrogates adopted the same tactics to stoke divisions and spread falsehoods that Russian provocateurs deployed so capably four years earlier. On Facebook, for example, the Trump campaign and its allies  repeatedly purchased ads that spread conspiracy theories about Biden’s son.
  Incensed, Biden and his campaign spent months imploring Facebook to crack down. Democrats attacked the company repeatedly for allowing Republicans to  spread falsehoods online without submitting the ads to fact checking. Even after the company tweaked its ad systems, Facebook did not follow all of Democrats’ demands, leaving Biden to pledge to take regulatory aim at the tech industry if he won the election.
      With that victory in hand, Biden and his aides have sought to lay the early groundwork for an administration that can meet its past promises. The team has signaled an interest in rethinking  Section 230, long considered Silicon Valley’s legal Holy Grail. The decades-old law spares a wide array of sites and services, including newspapers and social networks, from being held liable for the content posted by their users. The president-elect and his allies, however, contend that the law also has essentially allowed large technology companies to skirt accountability for their actions.
  Biden at one point called on Congress during the campaign to repeal the law in full. Not even a month after his election, one of Biden’s top new advisers signaled at an event at Georgetown Law that the incoming administration isn’t backing down on its plans. Bruce Reed, the White House’s  incoming deputy chief of staff, stressed publicly that it is “long past time to hold social media companies accountable for what’s published” on their sites and services.
    Sen. Richard Blumenthal (D-Conn.), who co-sponsored a bill to revise Section 230, said he has spoken “extensively” with the Biden transition team about revisiting the rules and adopting “other steps to force greater responsibility on these big tech companies.” Those conversations transpired in the weeks before rioters acting on Trump’s viral online falsehoods stormed the U.S. Capitol, leaving one police officer and four rioters dead — a tragedy that Blumenthal said could further embolden the White House and Congress to act.
  “I think the new Congress and new administration will share a very intense focus on reform,” he said.
    Facebook, in particular, has  signaled that it is open to regulation: Its chief executive, Mark Zuckerberg, told lawmakers last year that he even supports changes to Section 230 — although people familiar with the company’s thinking say they recognize that Congress may be hamstrung by the complexities of regulating political and hate speech. But the tech giant still faces formidable challenges in a Democrat-dominant Washington, a political reality that perhaps prompted the company to take  once-unfathomable disciplinary action to ban Trump earlier this month.
  “I think we’re looking at all the options here,” said Rep. Pramila Jayapal (D-Wash.), faulting Facebook and others for “opportunistic” moves to punish Trump online “two weeks before the next administration is going to come in.”
    “I think there have been plenty of hints that we, Democrats and Republicans and the next administration, are going to take very seriously the unregulated power of social media companies," she added.
  “For a while, the tech industry took Democrats for granted,” said Nu Wexler, a former Facebook, Twitter and Google communications official. “Now they confront a new political reality. Tech will still be in the crosshairs. But it will be for more substantive things like privacy, data collection and competition.”
    Facebook made an effort to hire and elevate conservatives at its Washington office over the past few years, to try to curry favor with the Trump administration. The company until this month — the day after Democrats regained control of Congress — had rarely sanctioned Trump, part of a strategy that sought to appease conservatives. Now, Facebook is shifting course and reevaluating its strategy, said people familiar with the company’s thinking who spoke on the condition of anonymity because they weren’t authorized to discuss the matter publicly. Facebook was one of the first major companies to freeze political donations to Republicans in the wake of the Capitol riot. Chief Operating Officer Sheryl Sandberg may play a larger role again, as she did during the Obama administration, two of the people said. Biden also has a longtime personal relationship with Nick Clegg, Facebook’s vice president for global affairs and communications, who was Britain’s deputy prime minister during the Obama years. Clegg was essentially Biden’s counterpart, and Biden once appeared on Clegg’s podcast.
  Although the Biden team has ties to big tech, outreach has been fairly limited, particularly when it comes to sharing concrete plans with the companies, two of the people say. Trump, by contrast, invited tech executives to New York for a meeting before his inauguration. President Barack Obama also made early outreach during his administration.
  But the expectation overall is that Biden will pick people who will be more aggressive in going after tech.
  The incoming Biden administration already counts some of Silicon Valley’s most effective critics among its ranks. Vanita Gupta, president of the Leadership Conference on Civil and Human Rights, and Kristen Clarke, president of the Lawyers’ Committee for Civil Rights Under Law, played instrumental roles in challenging Facebook, Google and Twitter — and are set to assume leadership roles at the Justice Department. In their current roles, Gupta and Clarke successfully pressured social media companies to take down harmful, false content, including posts, photos and videos that discouraged people from voting. They also waged landmark legal cases charging algorithmic discrimination and bias.
      “These hires are a good indication that the Biden administration is going to take tech seriously,” said Peter Romer-Friedman, a civil rights lawyer who has sued Facebook and worked with Gupta and Clarke’s organizations. “The last four years have shown Democratic leaders to be very skeptical of tech, which is very different from the Obama administration.”
      The incoming administration has not yet settled on its selections for other senior government positions overseeing the tech industry. That includes openings at the nation’s top competition enforcement agencies, a critical set of picks at a time when the government is suing  Facebook and Google, accusing them of violating federal antitrust laws. As Biden’s government prepares to inherit those cases, Democrats said they had encouraged the incoming administration to pick tough watchdogs in a bid to reverse the deregulatory streak of Democratic and Republican governments past.
  “I expect fairly strong antitrust enforcement from the administration,” said Rep. Ro Khanna (D-Calif.), whose Bay Area district includes tech companies such as Apple.
  On Capitol Hill, many congressional Democrats promised to forge ahead with their long-sought plans to bring Silicon Valley to heel now that they will have a supportive ally in the White House. The party’s one-vote margin in the Senate may be slim, but they think their frustration with Facebook, Google and Twitter is likely to be bipartisan.
  Sen. Ron Wyden (Ore.) pledged to advance new regulations around artificial intelligence to ensure that powerful, behind-the-scenes algorithms don’t exhibit bias on the basis of race or gender. Sen. Brian Schatz (Hawaii) said he plans to try to revive his bill tackling online privacy. And Rep. Frank Pallone Jr. (N.J.), the leader of the tech-focused House Energy and Commerce Committee, said he intends to hold “online platforms accountable for the disinformation and misinformation that threatens our Republic.” That includes an investigation that will touch on the role social media sites played in stoking the Capitol riot, he said.
  “We can actually work on these issues rather than use them as a talking point to hit each other over the head,” Schatz said about the political push to come. “The silver lining about the last four years, from a tech policy standpoint, is that there’s been a lot of thoughtful work. … We have no shortage of good ideas.”
title: 在拜登执政期间，科技巨头正准备加强严格的监管，因为在国会大厦袭击之后，民主党人对监管的呼吁变得更加紧迫

彭博社报道说，亚马逊已经永久关闭了其Prime Pantry服务，这是一种杂货和其他家庭必需品的送货服务。这项服务使购物者能够以每月5美元的费用购买零食，食品，清洁产品，饮料，早餐和其他日用品等产品。去年，当冠状病毒大流行在美国爆发时，亚马逊暂停了Prime Pantry，因为该公司无法跟上消费者订单的高需求。取而代之的是，亚马逊鼓励购物者通过亚马逊生鲜，全食市场或杂货和家用产品类别的亚马逊市场购买商品。
  Prime Pantry于2014年推出，与Amazon Fresh类似，它为Prime会员提供35美元或更多的订单免费送货服务。在某些地区，非Prime会员还可以按每张订单5.99美元的运费购买Prime Pantry。 Prime Pantry购物者可以在美国境内购买，也可以购买Shop＆amp;以五项的任意组合保存项以节省5％，或以十项的任意组合保存项以节省10％。
 在2018年，该服务从每笔订单运输费用5.99美元转变为月度会员制。 Prime会员每月只需支付5美元，就可以跳过“每笔订单”费用。那些不想订阅的人可以选择退出并为每票货支付8美元。
  亚马逊发言人在给彭博社的电子邮件中解释了该公司终止Prime Pantry的理由，“作为我们致力于提供最佳客户体验的承诺的一部分，我们决定将Amazon Pantry的选择转移到主要的Amazon.com商店，以便客户可以无需额外订购或购买即可更快地获得日常家用产品。”
 彭博社说，Prime Pantry计划的成员在12月收到通知并获得退款。
   购物者可以通过亚马逊的Subscribe＆amp; amp; amp; amp; quot;订阅者可以节省多达15％的费用，并在购买五种或以上符合条件的产品后自动免运费。尊贵会员最多可以节省20％。在下订单之前，亚马逊会向购物者发送提醒，让他们知道哪些商品，价格和折扣适用。然后，购物者可以选择继续交付，跳过交付或完全取消订阅。没有参加费用。企业帐户不符合“订阅＆amp;保存程序。
 购物者还可以通过杂货店销售和购买商品。交易页面，通过常规的亚马逊市场（搜索“食品杂货”中的3,000多种最畅销产品），或通过提货或送货方式进入亚马逊新鲜食品和全食市场。 
COVID改变了每个人的生活，因此去年春天，当亚马逊发现它无法满足对家庭必需品的高需求时，这并不奇怪。 尽管该公司迅速加快了速度，增加了员工，并扩大了分销和交付方式，但他们可能发现，除了提供其他服务外，提供Prime Pantry的成本效益也越来越低。 如果购物者可以通过其他亚马逊渠道获得相同的产品，那么这一变化就不算什么重大的变化，特别是对于Prime会员而言。 订阅＆amp; 保存是重复购买的便捷选择，而亚马逊新鲜食品和全食市场则适合新鲜食品和家庭必需品。

Amazon has permanently shut down its Prime Pantry service, a delivery service for groceries and other household essentials,  reports Bloomberg. The service allowed shoppers to buy products like snacks, pantry items, cleaning products, beverages, breakfast items and other household products in bulk for a $5 per month fee. When the coronavirus pandemic started in the U.S. last year,  Amazon put a pause on Prime Pantry because the company could not keep up with the high demand of consumer orders. Instead, Amazon encouraged shoppers to purchase their items through Amazon Fresh, Whole Foods Market or the Amazon marketplace in the grocery and household product categories.
  Prime Pantry launched in 2014 and, similar to Amazon Fresh, it offered free shipping on orders of $35 or more to Prime members. In select areas, non-Prime members could also shop Prime Pantry for a $5.99 shipping fee per order. Available in the contiguous United States, Prime Pantry shoppers could purchase Shop & Save items in any combination of five items to save 5%, or in any combination of 10 items to save 10%.
 In 2018, the service moved away from the $5.99 per order shipping fee to a  monthly membership model. For $5 per month, Prime members could skip the “per order” fee. Those who did not want to subscribe could opt out and pay $8 per shipment instead.
  In an email to Bloomberg, an Amazon spokesperson explained the company’s rationale for discontinuing Prime Pantry, “As part of our commitment to delivering the best possible customer experience, we have decided to transfer Amazon Pantry selection to the main Amazon.com store so customers can get everyday household products faster, without an extra subscription or purchase requirement.”
 Members who were part of the Prime Pantry program were notified in December and received refunds, Bloomberg says.
   Instead of Prime Pantry, shoppers can purchase their groceries and household goods through  Amazon’s Subscribe & Save program, a recurring delivery option where subscribers can save up to 15% and get free shipping when they buy five or more eligible products to be auto-delivered to one address. Prime members can save as much as 20%. Prior to order deliveries, Amazon sends reminders to shoppers to let them know what items, pricing and discounts apply. Shoppers can then choose to continue with the delivery, skip it or cancel the subscription altogether. There are no fees to participate. Business accounts are not eligible for the Subscribe & Save program.
 Shoppers can also purchase items in through the  Grocery Sales & Deals page, through the regular Amazon marketplace (search on “pantry staples” for more than 3,000 best-selling products), or Amazon Fresh and Whole Foods Market via pick-up or delivery.
   COVID changed life for everyone, so it was not a surprise last spring when Amazon found it could not keep up with the high demand for household essentials. Though the company quickly got up to speed, adding staff and expanding distribution and delivery options, they likely found that it was no longer cost effective to offer Prime Pantry in addition to its other services. If shoppers can get the same products through other Amazon avenues, this change is not a significant one, particularly for Prime members. Subscribe & Save is a convenient alternative for recurring purchases, while Amazon Fresh and Whole Food Market are good for fresh groceries and household essentials.
title: 亚马逊永久关闭Prime Pantry
title_en: Amazon permanently shuts down Prime Pantry

在本月初从应用商店中删除了Parler之后，一个位于华盛顿的非营利组织上周日起诉苹果，要求苹果从其平台上禁止流行的消息传递应用Telegram。
  “更安全的网络联盟”组织首次由《华盛顿邮报》报道，认为电报已成为极端主义者在网络上传播其思想的场所。
 提起诉讼的是一个更安全的网络联盟，一个倡导旨在从社交媒体中删除极端主义内容的技术和政策的无党派组织，以及联盟的主席，前美国驻摩洛哥大使马克·金斯伯格。
 根据诉讼，无党派团体指控Telegram主持“白人至上主义者，新纳粹分子和其他仇恨内容的对话”，这被视为违反了App Store的服务条款。未来几天，Google也将提起类似诉讼。
 该诉讼已向北加州美国地方法院提起，并要求苹果从App Store中删除Telegram，就像该公司本月与Parler所做的一样。 Parler以允许用户共享煽动暴力的内容而无需过滤器而闻名，苹果公司禁止iOS应用程序，称该应用程序代表“暴力威胁和非法活动”。
 尽管Telegram是一个消息传递应用程序，但它还提供了通道和公共组，用户可以通过共享的URL或使用应用程序的内置搜索来访问它们。目前尚不清楚苹果是否会在应用商店中禁止Telegram，但Telegram创始人Pavel Durov已经提到，如果苹果从iOS禁止该应用，他们正在“开发功能丰富的Web应用，该应用将在Safari中运行”。

note_en:
Following the removal of Parler from the App Store earlier this month, a Washington-based non-profit group sued Apple last Sunday asking the company to ban the popular messaging app Telegram from its platform.
  First reported by the   Washington Post, the “Coalition for a Safer Web” group argues that Telegram has become a place for extremists to spread their ideas on the web.
 Filing the suit was the Coalition for a Safer Web, a nonpartisan group that advocates for technologies and policies to remove extremist content from social media, and the coalition’s president, Marc Ginsberg, a former U.S. ambassador to Morocco.
 According to the lawsuit, the non-partisan group accuses Telegram of hosting conversations of “white supremacists, neo-Nazis, and other hateful content,” which would be considered a violation of App Store’s terms of service. A similar suit will be filed against Google in the next few days.
 The suit was filed in the U.S. District Court for Northern California and requires Apple to remove Telegram from the App Store,  just as the company did with Parler this month. Parler is known for allowing users to share content inciting violence without filters, and Apple banned the iOS app claiming that the app represents “threats of violence and illegal activity.”
 Although Telegram is a messaging app, it also offers channels and public groups that users can access through a shareable URL or using the app’s built-in search. It’s still unclear whether Apple will ban Telegram from the App Store, but Telegram founder Pavel Durov has already mentioned that they’re “working on a feature-rich web app which will run in Safari” in case Apple bans the app from iOS.
  You’re reading 9to5Mac — experts who break news about Apple and its surrounding ecosystem, day after day. Be sure to check out  our homepage for all the latest news, and follow 9to5Mac on  Twitter,  Facebook, and  LinkedIn to stay in the loop. Don’t know where to start? 因此，我为他们的单推进剂申请了Rocket Labs的专利，这种推进剂可以或可以不用于光子卫星总线
  综上所述：单一推进剂由固体氧化剂颗粒，提高燃烧温度的金属颗粒组成，是主要的燃烧物质（热剂）以及结合液体和溶解气体的混合物，它们起着推进剂质量和转动作用的作用。通常是非牛顿流体或胶体中的简单固体燃料。该凝胶通过加压气体推向膀胱膜或可以机械摇动的金属活塞（就像填塞枪一样）推动。通过精心选择表面活性剂，粘合剂和流变改性剂，使其具有非牛顿流体性质，因此可以将其推入雾化器，喷入燃烧室并燃烧而不会燃烧回吹（仅在雾化时燃烧，因此燃烧前沿无法爬回管线并进入油箱并炸毁所有东西）。溶解在其中的气体很可能是氮氧化物，可以被燃烧室加热并送回以压缩燃料箱。
 这东西是相当先进的！比肼（标准单燃）更安全，密度更高，高达1.72克/毫升！与目前正在飞行的绿色推进剂注入任务中正在飞行的离子溶液AF-M315E等其他先进的单推进剂相比，Rocketlabs燃料具有更高的密度（〜1.72对1.47），并且可能具有更高的Isp，也许是未知的，我找不到火箭实验室报告其燃料的Isp。同样与AF-M31E不同的是，这些东西不能至少用作燃烧室冷却剂，至少不能直接用作燃烧室的冷却剂，它的气体可以被加热并作为增压器送回或放出以吸收芯吸热量。
 我们现在看到的是取代标准的单推进剂肼的举动，它将是RocketLab的VLM，将是NASA AF-M31E还是ESA的LMP-103S？ LMP-103S的Isp和密度低于AF-M31E，但是VLM的密度甚至更高，ISP甚至更高。专利要求火箭实验室的测试火箭使用30％的载流子，如果我们将载流子作为标准固体火箭燃料中的粘合剂，则Isp将使Isp介于250-260之间。但是专利允许粘合剂（载体流体）的类型和浓度可能会使ISP更高：如果以二硝酰胺铵为氧化剂，粘合剂/流体的10-15％会使Isp超过300。
   由RuBisCO编辑

So I came upon Rocket Labs patent for their monopropellant, the one they may or may not have flown on their  Photon Satellite Bus
  To sum it up: the monopropellant consist of particles of solid oxidizer, particles of a metal to raise the temperature of combustion and be the primary combusted material (thermic agent) and a mix of binding liquids and dissolved gasses that act as propellant mass and turn what would normally be a simple solid fuel into a Non-Newtonian fluid or gell. This gell is pushed either via pressurized gas against a bladder membrane or a metalic piston that can be cranked mechanically (just like a caulk gun). Thanks to its Non-Newtonian fluid properties via the careful choice of surfactants, binders, Rheology Modifiers, it can be pushed into an atomizer, sprayed into the combustion chamber and burn without combustion blow back (it only combusts when atomized and thus the combustion front can't crawl back up the line and into the fuel tank and blow everything up). The gas dissolve in it is most likely Nitrogen Oxide which can be heated by the combustion chamber and sent back to compress the fuel tank.
 This stuff is pretty advance! Much safer then Hydrazine (stardard monopropllant) and way denser, up to 1.72 g/ml! Compared to other advance monopropellants such as ionic solution  AF-M315E which is flying right now on  Green Propellant Infusion Mission which was just launched into orbit, Rocketlabs fuel has higher density ( ~1.72 vs 1.47) and potentially has higher Isp, maybe, not known, nowhere can I find rocketlab reporting the Isp of their fuel. Also unlike AF-M31E this stuff can't be used as combustion chamber coolant, not directly at least, the gas in its can be heated and sent back as pressurizer or bleed off to wick heat.
 What we see now is a move to replace hydazine, the standard monopropellant, will it be RocketLab's VLM, will it be NASA AF-M31E, or the ESA's  LMP-103S? LMP-103S has less Isp and density than AF-M31E, but VLM has even higher density and maybe even higher  ISP. Rocket labs test rocket is claimed by the patent to use 30% carrier-fluid that would put the Isp somewhere between 250-260 if we count the carrier-fluid as binder in a standard solid rocket fuel, but the patant allows for a range of binder (carrier-fluid) types and precentages that could, possible, bring the  ISP even higher: 10-15%  binder/fluid would bring the Isp to over 300 if ammonium dinitramide is the oxidizer.
   Edited   by RuBisCO

1月18日（路透社）-社交媒体网站和受美国极右翼欢迎的应用程序Parler在俄罗斯一家科技公司的帮助下部分返回在线。
 当用户呼吁暴力行为并发布视频以赞扬1月6日对美国国会大厦的袭击后，Parler被亚马逊公司的托管部门和其他合作伙伴放慢脚步，从互联网上消失了。
  周一，Parler的网站再次可以访问，尽管只有该网站首席执行官的消息说他正在努力恢复功能。
 基础设施专家罗纳德·吉尔梅特（Ronald Guilmette）告诉路透社，它使用的互联网协议地址由DDos-Guard拥有，DDos-Guard由两名俄罗斯人控制，并提供包括分布式拒绝服务攻击防护在内的服务。
 如果网站完全恢复，Parler用户将可以查看和发表评论。但是，大多数用户都喜欢该应用程序，但苹果公司和谷歌官方商店仍禁止使用该应用程序。
 Parler首席执行官John Matze和DDoS-Guard的代表未回复置评请求。
 Matze上周三告诉路透社，该公司正在与多家服务提供商进行谈判，但拒绝透露细节。 
DDoS-Guard与其他种族主义，右派和阴谋网站合作，这些网站被大规模杀人犯用来分享信息，包括8kun。它还为俄罗斯政府站点提供了支持。
 吉尔梅特说，DDoS-Guard的网站上以公司名称Cognitive Cloud LP列出了苏格兰的一个地址，但该地址由俄罗斯顿河畔罗斯托夫的两个人拥有。其中一位告诉《卫报》，他并不了解公司提供的所有内容。
 批评者认为，依靠一家俄罗斯公司存在潜在的安全风险，对于自称爱国者的网站来说，这是一个奇怪的选择。
 俄罗斯的宣传已经激起了美国的政治分歧，支持即将离任的美国总统唐纳德·特朗普，并放大了有关选举舞弊的虚假叙述，还抗议警察的野蛮行径。
 透露其拥有超过1200万用户的Parler上周一起诉了亚马逊，此前这家电子商务巨头和云服务提供商中断了服务，理由是对暴力电话的控制不力。 （由纽约肯尼思·李（Kenneth Li）和伊丽莎白·卡利福德（Elizabeth Culliford）和旧金山（San Francisco）的约瑟夫·曼（Joseph Menn）报道；林肯·费斯特（Lincoln Feast）编辑。

Jan 18 (Reuters) - Parler, a social media website and app popular with the American far right, has partially returned online with the help of a Russian-owned technology company.
 Parler vanished from the internet when dropped by Amazon Inc's hosting arm and other partners for poor moderation after its users called for violence and posted videos glorifying the Jan. 6 attack on the U.S. Capitol.
  On Monday, Parler's website was reachable again, though only with a message from its chief executive saying he was working to restore functionality.
 The internet protocol address it used is owned by DDos-Guard, which is controlled by two Russian men and provides services including protection from distributed denial of service attacks, infrastructure expert Ronald Guilmette told Reuters.
 If the website is fully restored, Parler users would be able to see and post comments. Most users prefer the app, however, which remains banned from the official Apple Inc and Google stores.
 Parler CEO John Matze and representatives of DDoS-Guard did not reply to requests for comment.
 Last Wednesday, Matze told Reuters the company was in talks with multiple service providers but declined to elaborate.
 DDoS-Guard has worked with other racist, rightist and conspiracy sites that have been used by mass murderers to share messages, including 8kun. It has also supported Russian government sites.
 DDoS-Guard's website lists an address in Scotland under the company name Cognitive Cloud LP, but that is owned by two men in Rostov-on-Don, Russia, Guilmette said. One of them told the Guardian recently that he was not aware of all of the content the company facilitates.
 Parler critics said it was a potential security risk for it to depend on a Russian company, as well as an odd choice for a site popular with self-described patriots.
 Russian propaganda has stoked political divisions in the United States, supporting outgoing U.S. President Donald Trump and amplifying false narratives about election fraud but also protests against police brutality.
 Parler, which disclosed it has over 12 million users, sued Amazon last Monday after the ecommerce giant and cloud services provider cut off service, citing poor moderation of calls to violence. (Reporting by Kenneth Li and Elizabeth Culliford in New York and Joseph Menn in San Francisco; Editing by Lincoln Feast.)
title: 在俄罗斯技术公司的支持下，Parler再次出现
title_en: Parler partially reappears with support from Russian technology firm

 ＆＃34;我们查看了那里的煽动暴力行为，＆＃34;库克在“福克斯新闻周日”告诉克里斯·华莱士。
  派勒（Parler）是深受保守派欢迎的替代性社交网络，近几个月来一直在迅速普及。但是该平台未能遏制充满仇恨的暴力言论，大型科技公司表示这可能导致另一次暴力袭击。苹果（AAPL）和谷歌（GOOGL）从其应用商店中启动了Parler，而亚马逊（AMZN）停止托管该服务。
 阻止数十亿人轻松访问社交网络的能力是一项沉重的责任-所有政治说辞的批评者都认为，这不属于少数经营着世界最大公司的百万富翁和亿万富翁的手中。苹果及其同伙禁止Parler的决定的一些批评家认为，将应用程序推离主流将使参与者进入互联网的黑暗渠道-并有可能进一步加剧激进现象。
  但是库克（Cook）反对说，托管每项服务，无论其内容如何，​​都是苹果的工作。他指出，苹果为其托管的200万个应用程序提供了服务条款，并且拒绝按规则运行的应用程序无法访问苹果的大量受众。
 我们显然无法控制互联网上的内容，但我们从未认为我们的平台应该是互联网上内容的简单复制， ;库克说。
 如果该应用程序有效地控制了用户，苹果将欢迎Parler（只要Parler找到一个新的云提供商来托管社交网络）。苹果首席执行官致辞。 
＆＃34;我们只暂停了它们，＆＃34;库克指出。 ＆＃34;如果他们在一起进行节制，他们就会回到那里。
  苹果公司的首席执行官批评了其他科技公司缺乏理想，包括牺牲了用户。通过追求利润来保护隐私。但是，全球市值最高的公司苹果必须谨慎行事。其庞大的规模和权力意味着任何有争议的举动都可能被监管机构起诉，这些监管机构已起诉其他大型高科技公司，包括Google和Facebook，因为它们违反了反托拉斯法。如果苹果因涉嫌滥用垄断权力而受到审查，则迫使其他公司屈服于它的论点不会变得更容易。
 库克周日仍然认为，经营一家科技公司远不止是赚大钱。他周日说，他认为苹果的使命应该是解决世界上一些最大的问题。公司及其员工一直在努力做正确的事-这一使命促使他每天早上上班。
 这有助于影响他对Parler的决定-特别是在国会大厦被围困之后。
 ＆＃34;那是我一生中最难过的时刻之一-看到袭击我们的国会大厦和袭击我们的民主，＆＃34;库克说。 ＆＃34;老实说，我感觉自己处于某种替代现实中。这不可能发生。＆＃34;
note_en:
New York (CNN Business)Apple, along with Amazon and Google, effectively  kicked Parler off the internet in the wake of the January 6 US Capitol siege. Despite criticism that Big Tech wields too much power over speech, Apple CEO Tim Cook defended his decision.
 "We looked at the incitement to violence that was on there," Cook told Chris Wallace on "Fox News Sunday."
  Parler, the alternative social network popular with conservatives, had been surging in popularity in recent months. But the platform failed to rein in  hate-filled, violent speech, which Big Tech companies said could lead to another violent attack.  Apple  (  AAPL) and  Google  (  GOOGL) booted Parler off their app stores and  Amazon  (  AMZN) stopped hosting the service.
 The ability to stop billions of people from easily accessing a social network is a weighty responsibility -- one that critics of all political persuasions have argued does not belong in the hands of a select few millionaires and billionaires running the world's largest companies. Some critics of Apple and its cohort's decisions to ban Parler have argued that pushing the app out of the mainstream will  drive participants to dark channels of the internet -- and potentially deeper down the rabbit hole of radicalization.
  But Cook disputed that it's Apple's job to host every service, regardless of its content. He noted that Apple has terms of service for the 2 million apps its hosts, and apps that refuse to play by the rules aren't allowed to access Apple's massive audience.
 "We obviously don't control what's on the internet, but we've never viewed that our platform should be a simple replication of what's on the internet," Cook said.
 Apple will welcome back Parler -- provided Parler finds a new cloud provider to host the social network -- if the app effectively moderates users' speech, said the Apple CEO.
 "We've only suspended them," Cook noted. "If they get their moderation together they would be back on there."
  Apple's CEO has criticized other tech companies for lacking ideals -- including sacrificing users' privacy by chasing profit. But Apple, the world's most valuable company, has to tread lightly. Its substantial size and power mean any controversial move can get  under the skin of regulators that have sued other Big Tech companies, including Google and Facebook, for violating antitrust law. Forcing other companies to bend to its will isn't going to make Apple's argument easier if it finds itself under scrutiny for allegedly abusing monopoly power.
 Still, Cook argued Sunday that running a tech company is about more than making big bucks. He said Sunday that he believes Apple's mission should be to solve some of the world's biggest problems. The company and its employees are always trying to do the right thing -- a mission that motivates him to come to work each morning.
 That helped influence his decision about Parler -- particularly in the wake of the Capitol siege.
 "It was one of the saddest moments of my life -- seeing an attack on our Capitol and an attack on our democracy," Cook said. "I felt like I was in some sort of alternate reality, to be honest with you. This could not be happening."
title: 蒂姆·库克（Tim Cook）：为什么我拒绝Parler离开Apple的App Store
title_en: Tim Cook: Why I Kicked Parler Off Apple's App Store

y轴将最近7天与最近3个月中该周的最差一周（在该州）进行比较。因此，例如，德克萨斯州（当前达到峰值）看起来像这样：因此，德克萨斯州的＆＃34; 1.0＆＃34;在y轴上，由于最近7天是最近12周（当前浪）以来的最高水平。纽约州，路易斯安那州，新泽西州，夏威夷州和许多其他州也都处于1.0。但是，某些州（例如伊利诺伊州）处于较低水平（在这种情况下为0.486）。
 请注意，德克萨斯州和伊利诺伊州的峰值并不完全相同。伊利诺伊州占总人口的百分比更高。不同的州有不同的人口，但人口密度也不同，例如，如果人们在同一栋公寓楼里挨着脸颊生活，就很难阻止这种流行病。因此，将每个州与该州的高峰（最差）周（过去12个周中的周）进行比较，其中1.0表示最近一周是他们所见过的最糟糕的一周，数字较低则意味着它已经好于那。这是北达科他州和南达科他州的样子。
    现在，总确诊病例数较高的州现在所看到的病例数有所减少（人均数量与最近三个月的峰值相比），这一事实本身并不令人惊讶。这就是流行病正常工作的方式。大火烧过大部分干火，然后熄灭。令人惊讶的是，我们已经看到这一数字低至达科他州的约12％。这么低的数字怎么会导致羊群免疫呢？
 这里应该提到的一件事是“畜群免疫”。与“淘汰牛群”无关，也不意味着“牛群”的每个成员都是免疫的。这意味着免疫人群的百分比对于流行病来说太高了，无法自我维持。如果另一个人生病了，那么暴露于该病毒的大多数人已经具有抗药性，并且没有足够的人生病以保持疾病的发展。有点像篝火后灰烬还没有全部被完全消耗掉；那里通常还有更多的燃料。但是有足够的东西变成了灰烬，表明火并不能自我维持。一旦种群达到了群免疫力，新的病毒暴露就会迅速消失。奇怪的是，这应该以低至12％的数字发生。
 一种可能是，先前的估计需要达到人群免疫力之前需要70％的人口抗药性，这完全是错误的。很大程度上取决于谁真正得到了它，以及是否是超级传播者。 （例如，从事服务行业工作的人在一天内与很多其他人互动）也是第一个获得该职位的人（这不是不合理的猜测），然后这个数字可能要低得多。如果您的12％包括所有潜在的超级传播者，那么12％也许就足够了。不过，这很难管理，我的猜测不是这里发生的事情。
 主要问题是，并非所有感染covid-19的人都能通过测试确认这一点。其中一些无症状，从不知道它们已被感染。有些人病得不是很重，也没有接受检查，因为没有理由让他们去（无论如何，他们只是要待在家里）。有些人害怕知道真相。如果他们经过多次测试并且每次测试都呈阳性，那么可能还会有一些人被计数一次以上。因此，我们需要一种方法来猜测已确认的案例（包括重复计算的案例）与实际案例（没有重复计算的案例）的比率。
 关于covid-19的实际死亡率的估计是有争议的，但是John Ioannidis发表了一篇论文，该论文收集了许多不同的血清阳性率研究，以估计约有0.23％的covid-19患者会因此而丧生。他指出，由于不同的压力或当地人口或医疗保健对策的差异，不同地区的情况可能有所不同，因此，我们估算的费用是美国的两倍，即0.46％。大约在收集数据的同时（4月和5月），美国每周确认病例为0.05％，每周死亡0.0015％。

 对这种比率进行某种形式的仔细检查会很好。幸运的是，斯洛伐克提供了一个。该国成为国际头条新闻，因为在10月的最后一个周末，斯洛伐克当局测试了该国几乎所有成年人口的冠状病毒。到本周末估计的400万目标人口中，共有360万人接受了测试，全国阳性率为1.06％。考虑到该测试（有必要便宜且能够大规模部署）不如常规测试那么准确，因此该比率可能高达2％。鉴于常规措施（“确诊病例”）约为0.25％，这将使我们得出的实际病例与确诊病例的比率在4-8之间。
 现在，这些数字都不是非常精确的，无论如何，我们不应该期望已确认病例与实际病例的比率在任何地方都相同。预期不同的政府和不同的医疗保健系统将给实际感染了covid-19的人带来不同的可能性，使其接受测试以确认这一点。但是，这两种方法都基于不同国家/地区的不同数据集，它们提供了相似的答案，这一事实令人鼓舞，因为我们处于领先地位。对于每个“确诊病例”，实际上有4-8个人感染了covid-19。
  如果人均确诊病例数为12.545％（北达科他州）或11.862％（南达科他州），则实际感染的人口百分比更像是70％。难怪他们看到这两个州的新病例急剧下降。但是其他州呢？
 在此页面的底部，您将看到所有州的表格（加上哥伦比亚特区，波多黎各等），以及已确诊的病例总数占总人口的比例。它也具有最近7天确诊病例的比率与该州在过去12周中经历的最坏一周的比率（即，它是否从最近的峰值下降，或者是否设置了新的纪录高点，在这种情况下，它将设为1.0）。但是，如果您想在一张图中查看它们的全部内容，以查看整体趋势，这里又是：
 请注意，不仅达科他州的人口数量非常少（最近），而且人均占有率约为10％的所有州都在下降（数字低于1）。实际上，一旦一个州的命中率达到8％，每周新案件的数量就会下降。如果8％的确诊病例意味着大约48％的人已经感染了这种病毒，这并不奇怪。
  好吧，这取决于您认为疫苗的用途。如果您认为这是结束当前的大流行的原因，并且我们都躲藏起来以便在出现疫苗之前不会被感染，那么是的，恐怕这也意味着疫苗也可能会出现来帮助您解决这一问题（特别是如果您必须在免疫之前同时服用两种剂量）。如果您居住在夏威夷，缅因州或佛蒙特州等州，确诊病例远低于达科他州的水平，那么即使在这种大流行中，这种疫苗也许也可以拯救一些人。但是，鉴于所有这些州都在经历新的确诊病例的记录或接近记录的水平，我对此表示怀疑。 
应该提到的是，目前北达科他州和南达科他州是推出疫苗的前三个州中的两个。但是，由于他们仅获得了5％的人口（并且仅计算一剂的人数），我怀疑这是推动近期下降的主要因素。但这肯定不会造成伤害。
 但是针对新病毒而开发的疫苗从未及时出现，可以对第一次大流行起到很大的帮助，因此，这并不是对那些开发这些疫苗的人们的真正打击。几十年来，我们已经患了麻疹，腮腺炎，风疹等，我们仍然需要接种疫苗，因为即使目前这三者均没有大流行（敲打木头），我们知道它们仍然在那里，并将最终返回。一旦在上一轮浪潮后出生的人足够提供“新鲜燃料”，疫苗将帮助我们避免每20年左右（对于每种病毒）重复整个大流行。为火。鉴于这种流行病使人们多么愚蠢和疯狂，我想我们都应该为此感到感激。
note_en:
The y axis, is comparing the most recent 7 days to the worst week (in that state) in the last 3 months. So, for example, Texas (currently peaking) looks like this:  Therefore, Texas has a "1.0" on the y-axis, since the most recent 7 days are the highest level seen in the last 12 weeks (the current wave). New York, Louisiana, New Jersey, Hawaii, and a lot of other states are also at 1.0. However, some states like Illinois are at a lower level (in this case, 0.486).
 Note that the peaks for Texas and Illinois are not quite the same; Illinois' was a little higher, as a percent of the population. Different states have different populations, but also different population densities, and it's harder to stop an epidemic if people are living cheek by jowl in the same apartment building, for example. So each state is compared to the peak (worst) week for that state (out of the last 12), with 1.0 meaning the most recent week is the worst they've seen, and lower numbers meaning that it has gotten better than that. Here's what North and South Dakota look like.
    Now, the fact that states with higher total confirmed cases, are seeing fewer cases now (per capita, compared to their peak in the last 3 months) is not in itself surprising. That's the way pandemics normally work. The fire burns through most of the dry tinder, and then it goes out. What is surprising is that we are seeing this already with a number as low as ~12%, as in the Dakotas. How can a number that low lead to herd immunity?
 One thing that should be mentioned here is that "herd immunity" has nothing to do with "culling the herd", and also does not mean that every single member of the "herd" is immune. What it means is that the percentage of the population that are immune, is too high for an epidemic to be self-sustaining. If another person gets sick, most of the people they expose to the virus are already resistant, and not enough people get sick to keep things going. It's kind of like how the ash left behind after a campfire is not all, completely 100% consumed; there is often still more fuel there. But enough of it was turned to ash, that the fire was not self-sustaining. Once a population has reached "herd immunity", new exposures to the virus sputter and die out quickly. The odd thing is, that this should happen with a number as low as 12%.
 One possibility, is that the previous estimates of needing 70% of the population to be resistant before you reach herd immunity, were simply incorrect. A lot depends on who exactly gets it, and if the people who are "super-spreaders" (people with service industry jobs who interact with a lot of other people in a day, for example) are also the first to get it (not an unreasonable guess), then the number could be a lot lower. If your 12% includes all the potential super-spreaders, maybe 12% is enough. That would be hard to manage, though, and my guess is that's not what's going on here.
 The main issue is that not all people who get infected with covid-19, get this confirmed with a test. Some of them are asymptomatic, and never know they are infected. Some are not very sick, and don't get tested because there's no reason for them to (they're just going to stay home, regardless). Some people are afraid to know the truth. There may also be a few people who get counted more than once, if they get tested multiple times and test positive each time. So, we need a way to guess at the ratio of confirmed cases (including those who are double-counted), to actual cases (with no double-counting).
 Estimates of the actual mortality rate for covid-19 are contentious, but  John Ioannidis published a paper which collected together many different seroprevalence studies, to estimate something like 0.23% of people who get covid-19 will die from it. He points out that it could be different for different regions, though, either because of different strains or differences in the local population or healthcare response, so let's take an estimate of twice that for the U.S., or 0.46%. At about the same time as the data was collected (April and May), the U.S. had .05% confirmed cases per week, and 0.0015% deaths per week.
 actual cases .000015 deaths------------ * --------------------- ===> 6.52 actual cases per confirmed case.0046 deaths .0005 confirmed cases
 It would be nice to have some kind of double-check of this ratio. Fortunately,  Slovakia provided one. "The country made international headlines as, over the last weekend of October, Slovak authorities tested almost all the country's adult population for coronavirus. A total of 3.6 million people out of an estimated 4 million target population were tested that weekend with a countrywide positivity rate of 1.06%." Given that the test was (necessarily, being cheap and capable of being deployed on such a massive scale) not as accurate as more conventional tests, the rate may have been as high as 2%. Given that conventional measures ("confirmed cases") was around 0.25%, that would give us a ratio of actual cases to confirmed cases somewhere in the range of 4-8.
 Now, neither of these numbers are super-precise, and in any case, we should not expect the ratio of confirmed cases to actual cases to be the same everywhere. Different governments and different healthcare systems would be expected to result in a different likelihood for a person who is actually infected with covid-19, to go get tested to confirm it. But the fact that both methods, based on different datasets in different countries, provide similar answers, is encouraging that we are somewhere in the ballpark. For every "confirmed case", we have something like 4-8 people who have actually been infected with covid-19.
  If the number of confirmed cases per capita is 12.545% (North Dakota) or 11.862% (South Dakota), then the actual percentage of the population that have been infected is more like 70%. No wonder they have seen a precipitous decline in new cases in both states. But what about other states?
 At the bottom of this web page, you will see a table of all of the states (plus D.C., Puerto Rico, etc.), and their total confirmed cases as a fraction of their total population. It also has the ratio of the most recent 7 days confirmed cases, to the worst week that state has had in the last 12 weeks (i.e. is it currently declining from its recent peak, or is it setting new record highs in which case it will be at 1.0). But if you want to look at them all in one graph, to see the overall trend, here it is again:
 Note that, not only are the Dakotas very low (recently), but also all of the states with something like 10% cases per capita, are declining (numbers lower than 1). In fact, it appears that once a state hits 8%, it will start to have declining numbers of new cases each week. If 8% confirmed cases means something more like 48% of population having been already infected with the virus, this is not too surprising.
  Well, it depends on what you think the vaccine is for. If you think it is to end the current pandemic, and we were all hiding out so that we would not get infected until the vaccine showed up, then yes, I'm afraid it means that the vaccine is probably showing up just too late to help with that (especially if you have to get both doses before you are immune). If you live in a state like Hawaii, Maine, or Vermont, with a total confirmed cases far below the levels of the Dakotas, then perhaps the vaccine may save some people even in this pandemic. Given that all of these states are experiencing record or near-record levels of new confirmed cases, though, I have my doubts.
 It should be mentioned that currently North and South Dakota are two of the top three states in getting the vaccine rolled out. However, since they have only gotten about 5% of the population (and that's counting people who have only gotten one dose), I doubt that it is the main factor driving the recent decline. But it sure doesn't hurt.
 But vaccines developed in response to a new virus have never showed up in time to help much with the first pandemic, so that is not really a knock on the people who developed these. We have had measles, mumps, rubella, etc. for many decades, and we still have to get vaccinated, because even though there's no pandemic of those three currently (knock on wood), we know they are still out there, and will eventually return. Vaccines help us to avoid repeating this whole pandemic every 20 years or so (for every virus), once there are enough people born after the last wave to provide "fresh fuel" for the fire. Given how stupid and crazy this pandemic has made people, I think we can all be thankful for that.
title: 我认为达科他州已经在畜群免疫中
title_en: I think the Dakotas are already at herd immunity

The new round, a Series C, for the Hyderabad-based startup was led by Salesforce Ventures, the venture arm of the American software giant. This is Salesforce Ventures’ one of rare investments in India. Existing investors including Lightspeed India and Sequoia Capital India also participated in the round, which brings the five-year old startup’s total raise to date to $35 million.
 Over 500 firms including — Tokopedia, Indorama, JG Summit Group, Zilingo, Zalora, Fave, Adani, Mahindra, Kotak, TVS, NSE, Ujjivan Small Finance Bank, Dr.Reddy’s, Nivea, Puma, Swiggy, Bigbasket — use  Darwinbox’s HR platform to provide more than a million employees of theirs with a range of features including insurance and early salary as loans in 60 nations, up from about 200 firms across 50 nations in 2019, said Chaitanya Peddi, co-founder of Darwinbox, in an interview with TechCrunch.
  Peddi said the startup has always looked up to Salesforce, and investment from the enterprise giant is “nothing sort of a child receiving validation from their father,” he said.
 The fundraise caps the most successful year for the startup that started with uncertainty as the coronavirus spread across Asian nations. The startup took a hit as its customers scrambled to navigate through the global pandemic, but the last two quarters have been its best to date, said Peddi. Overall, the startup’s revenue has ballooned by 300% since September 2019, when it  last raised money, he said. “In HR tech and SaaS space, we are now only behind SAP and Oracle in India in terms of revenue,” he said.
 Dev Khare, a partner at Lightspeed India, said that Darwinbox has become the preferred human capital management solution for Asian conglomerates, governments, and high-growth businesses and multi-national corporations operating in Asia as they witness digital transformation.
  Darwinbox’s platform is built to take care of the entire “hiring to retiring” cycle needs for employees. It handles onboarding of new hires, keeps a tap on their performance, monitors attrition rate, and provides an ongoing feedback loop.
 It also provides its customers with a social network for their employees to remain connected with one another and an AI assistant to apply for a leave or set up meetings with quick voice commands from their phones.
 Peddi said the startup will deploy the fresh capital to expand to several more countries, especially in more emerging markets in the Middle East Asia and Africa, and broaden its offerings. “We will be leveraging the power of our platform to do a lot more. We are a product-led firm and our focus will remain on innovation in that space,” he said. The startup is also open to exploring opportunities to acquire smaller firms for inorganic growth, he said.
 “India is home to one of the world’s youngest population, and by 2050, it is expected to account for over 18% of the global working age population,” said Arundhati Bhattacharya, Chairperson and CEO, Salesforce India, in a statement. “This makes technology platforms like Darwinbox, that focuses on workforces, incredibly important. I’m proud that Salesforce is supporting Darwinbox on their journey as they continue to grow and innovate in this space.”
 Alex Kayyal, partner and head of international at Salesforce Ventures, told TechCrunch in an interview that the firm helps its partners in a number of ways, including exposing them to the firm’s customers, executives and their networks, and helping startups scale their business. “We have one of the most innovative and disruptive customer bases that are looking for cloud solutions and digital transformation. So the opportunity to expose companies like Darwinbox to our customer base is something we get really excited about,” said Kayyal, adding that the firm is exploring several more deals in India.

note_en:
Colorize is a website that turns any word or phrase into a hex color. It works by using a search engine to find image results for the word or phrase, and then finding the average color across the approximately 25 image results. Because of this, there's usually a small delay when a word is searched for the first time.
    If you've ever been drawing in Photoshop and thought 'What color is the sky', or have been designing a website and looking for that perfect 'purple' color, or just wanted to know what color sand is, Colorize is the tool for you.

CBS新闻报道强调的一对新研究表明，诸如Apple Watch这样的智能手表可以在症状发作或呈阳性反应之前帮助检测COVID-19。这项由纽约西奈山卫生系统和加利福尼亚州斯坦福大学分别进行的研究使专家们希望，Apple Watch可以帮助“在阻止流行病和其他传染病中发挥重要作用”。
  西奈山（Mount Sinai）进行的研究发现，Apple Watch能够在COVID-19症状或阳性试验出现之前的7天之内检测到“个人心跳的细微变化”。该研究分析了心率变异性或心跳之间的时间变化，并纳入了近300名4月29日至9月29日之间佩戴Apple Watch的医护人员。
 报告解释说，这是衡量人体免疫系统运作状况的常用方法。
 “我们的目标是在感染时或人们知道自己生病之前使用工具来识别感染，”纽约西奈山伊坎医学院的医学助理教授，《勇士观察》的作者罗伯·赫尔滕说。研究。
 赫滕告诉CBS MoneyWatch：“我们已经知道，心率变异性标记会随着体内炎症的发展而变化，而Covid是令人难以置信的炎症事件。” “它使我们能够预测人们在被感染之前就已被感染。”
 “目前，我们依靠人们说他们病了并且感觉不适，但是佩戴Apple Watch并不需要任何积极的用户输入，并且可以识别出可能没有症状的人。这是更好地控制传染病的一种方式。”赫滕说。
 同时，斯坦福大学的另一项研究于11月发布，其结果包括来自Garmin，Fitbit和Apple的活动跟踪器。研究发现，这些设备可能表明冠状病毒阳性患者“在症状发作前最多九天半”的静息心率变化。 
研究称，研究人员能够在症状出现前四到七天发现近三分之二的COVID-19病例。
 该团队还创建了一个警报系统，以警告佩戴者其心率持续升高。
 负责这项研究的斯坦福大学教授迈克尔·斯奈德说：“我们将警报设置为一定的灵敏度，以便每两个月左右响一次。” “定期的波动不会触发警报，只有持续的重大变化。”
 他补充说：“这很重要，因为它提醒人们不要出去与人见面。”例如，当Snyder的闹钟最近响起时，他取消了面对面的会面，以防他被传染。
 Snyder继续解释说，这种技术可以帮助弥补测试策略中的缺陷。他解释说：“问题是，您无法一直对人进行[测试]，而这些设备只能以24/7的速度进行测量。”
 与其他已委托开展类似研究的智能手表和可穿戴公司（例如Oura Health和Whoop）不同，Apple没有资助或参与任何一项研究。

A pair of new studies highlighted  by a  CBS News report indicate that smartwatches such as the Apple Watch can help detect COVID-19 before the onset of symptoms or a positive test. The studies, separately undertaken by Mount Sinai Health System in New York and Stanford University in California, are giving experts hope that the Apple Watch can help “play a vital role in stemming the pandemic and other communicable diseases.”
  The research  performed by Mount Sinai found that the Apple Watch is able to detect “subtle changes in an individual’s heartbeat” up to seven days before the onset of COVID-19 symptoms or a positive test. The study analyzed heart rate variability, or the variation in time between heartbeats, and included nearly 300 health care workers who wore Apple Watches between April 29 and September 29.
 This is a commonly-used measure of how well a person’s immune system is working, the report explains.
 “Our goal was to use tools to identify infections at time of infection or before people knew they were sick,” said Rob Hirten, assistant professor of medicine at the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai in New York City and author of the Warrior Watch study.
 “We already knew that heart rate variability markers change as inflammation develops in the body, and Covid is an incredibly inflammatory event,” Hirten told CBS MoneyWatch. “It allows us to predict that people are infected before they know it.”
 “Right now, we rely on people saying they’re sick and not feeling well, but wearing an Apple Watch doesn’t require any active user input and can identify people who might be asymptomatic. It’s a way to better control infectious diseases,” Hirten said.
 Meanwhile, a separate study from  Stanford, the results of which were released in November, included activity trackers from Garmin, Fitbit, and Apple. The study found that these devices could indicate changes in resting heart rate “up to nine and a half days prior to the onset of symptoms” in coronavirus-positive patients.
 The researchers were able to identify nearly two-thirds of COVID-19 cases four to seven days before symptoms, the study says.
 The team also has created an alarm system that alerts wearers that their heart rate has been elevated for a sustained period of time.
 “We set the alarm with a certain sensitivity so it will go off every two months or so,” said Stanford University Professor Michael Snyder, who led the study. “Regular fluctuations won’t trigger the alarm — only significant, sustained changes will.”
 “It’s a big deal because it’s alerting people not to go out and meet people,” he added. When Snyder’s alarm recently went off, for instance, he cancelled an in-person meeting in case he might be infectious.
 Snyder went on to explain that this type of technology can help make up for flaws in testing strategies. “The problem is you can’t do [testing] on people all the time, whereas these devices measure you 24/7,” he explained.
 Apple did not fund or participate in either of these studies, unlike other smartwatch and wearable companies that have commissioned similar studies, such as Oura Health and Whoop.
 The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention  published a model last week indicating how the Apple Watch and other smartwatches can help curb the spread of COVID-19 by asymptomistc carriers.
  You’re reading 9to5Mac — experts who break news about Apple and its surrounding ecosystem, day after day. Be sure to check out  our homepage for all the latest news, and follow 9to5Mac on  Twitter,  Facebook, and  LinkedIn to stay in the loop. Don’t know where to start? Check out our  exclusive stories,  reviews,  how-tos, and  subscribe to our YouTube channel
title: 最新研究表明Apple Watch可以在症状和测试之前帮助检测Covid-19
title_en: New studies show Apple Watch can help detect Covid-19 before symptoms and tests

在佛罗伦萨著名的中世纪大教堂工作的工程师惊讶地发现，在百年历史的屋顶瓦片上有数百个动物脚印。
  该小组正在对覆盖圣塔玛丽亚·德尔菲奥雷中世纪大教堂的屋顶和各个圆顶的数十万个赤陶砖进行维护工作。
   专家们现在正忙着一张一张地绘制它们并在艰苦的过程中对其进行分类。
 Samuele Caciagli是负责大教堂维护技术方面的建筑师。
 ＆＃34;我们发现了数百种野生和家养动物的照片，更具体地说是狗和猫，小鸟，一些狐狸或貂的照片，＆＃34;他解释。
 看起来这些生物并不是最初建造它们时就行走在兵马俑上的，而不是无畏的动物将建筑物缩放到屋顶上行走。
  技术团队的Carlo Sacconi表示，这些瓷砖都是在1400年代初期在佛罗伦萨南部以意大利赤土瓦工厂而闻名的意大利小镇因普鲁内塔（Impruneta）上制成的，然后将其露天晾干，然后用炉子烘烤。 
随着软土的干燥，野生动物和家养动物在地砖上行走并留下了脚印。  任何想要查看瓷砖和版画示例的人都不必爬上建筑物的屋顶，而只需参观大教堂博物馆即可。  ＆＃34;瓷砖有八个模具，有两种不同的形状，一种是可以追溯到古罗马维特鲁威时代的经典矩形形状，＆＃34; 丽塔·菲拉迪（Rita Filardi）博物馆的负责收藏的女士解释说。
note_en:
Engineers working on Florence's famous medieval cathedral were surprised to find hundreds of animal footprints on the centuries-old roof tiles.
  The team were carrying out maintenance work on the hundreds of thousands of terracotta tiles covering the roofs and various domes of the medieval cathedral of Santa Maria del Fiore.
   Experts are now busy mapping them one by one and cataloguing them in a painstaking process.
 Samuele Caciagli is the architect in charge of technical aspects of maintenance at the cathedral.
 "We found hundreds of prints of both wild and domestic animals, more specifically, dogs and cats, small birds, some foxes or martens," he explained.
 Rather than intrepid animals scaling the building to walk on the roof, it seems the creatures walked across the terracottas when they were originally made.
  Carlo Sacconi from the technical team said the tiles, which were all made in the early 1400s in Impruneta, a small Italian town south of Florence famous for its terracotta tile factories, were put out to dry in the open air before being baked in furnaces.
 As the soft clay was drying, wild and domestic animals walked over the tiles and left their prints.
 Anyone who wants to see examples of the tiles and the prints does not have to climb on to the building's roof, they can simply visit the  cathedral museum instead.
 "There are eight moulds for the tiles, in two different shapes, one is the classic rectangular shape dating back to the ancient Roman times of Vitruvius," explained the woman in charge of the collection at the museum, Rita Filardi.
title: 在佛罗伦萨的中世纪大教堂屋顶上发现了数百种动物脚印
title_en: Hundreds of animal footprints found on Florence's medieval cathedral roof

Wi-Fi 6E正在非常缓慢地运用于您附近的产品。 Wi-Fi联盟从1月7日开始对设备进行认证，而CES 2021上看到了许多与Wi-Fi 6E推出有关的产品公告。
 如果您没有听说过，Wi-Fi 6E是去年FCC批准的Wi-Fi新标准。 Wi-Fi 6（否，即802.11ax）是一堆技术改进，主要旨在更有效地利用现有频谱，而Wi-Fi 6E则是将Wi-Fi扩展到新释放的频谱。以前，Wi-Fi仅在2.4Ghz和5GHz频谱上工作，但是Wi-Fi 6E使用6GHz频谱。在美国，6E具有大量的连续频谱-1200MHz。以前，5GHz仅提供140MHz的有用非DFS频谱，而2.4GHz仅具有70MHz的非常拥挤的频谱，这很容易受到运行中的微波和其他干扰的影响。
 Wi-Fi 6或Wi-Fi 6E都不是更快的速度-两者都更多地涉及处理Wi-Fi容量问题，这些问题经常在公寓楼和大型公共聚会中浮出水面。如果由于人口稠密地区的电波拥挤，您的Wi-Fi当前很糟糕，则Wi-Fi 6E可以极大地改善您的无线性能。但是，获得Wi-Fi 6E意味着要购买新的客户端和新的接入点，因此，本文总结如下。
  在CES上最引人注目的设备是三星Galaxy S21，而更便宜的变体没有Wi-Fi 6E，而价格为1200美元的Ultra>。型号确实如此，并且在大多数情况下，它是世界上第一部Wi-Fi 6E手机。预计2021年大多数Android手机都将保持这种趋势，而Snapdragon 888 SoC上将选择使用Wi-Fi 6E，它将最终出现在大多数旗舰设备中，并且您可能会看到更昂贵的机型采用它。
 Wi-Fi 6E也将用于笔记本电脑。 MSI的GE76 Raider看起来将成为第一款Wi-Fi 6E笔记本电脑，这要归功于Intel的AX210 Wi-Fi卡。顺便说一下，英特尔AX210卡已向用户出售，因此您现在可以使用附加卡自己将大多数台式机和笔记本电脑升级到Wi-Fi 6E。客户来了，所以我们需要的是Wi-Fi 6E接入点，即“路由器”。当您谈论多合一消费级网络设备时。大多数大型消费网络品牌都通过WLAN 6E路由器亮相CES。
     我们从最接近一般可用性的路由器开始：华硕ROG Rapture GT-AXE11000。这是9月份宣布的，它是全球第一款Wi-Fi 6E游戏路由器，但它终于有了一个发布窗口：2021年1月，Newegg目前可以预订，价格为549.99美元，发布日期为1月29日，但一些报道称该路由器已经发货。就像许多游戏一样路由器，GT-AXE1100具有疯狂的设计，看起来它可以翻转并随时像蜘蛛一样四处走动。
    除了背面通常的4个千兆位LAN端口和一个千兆位WAN端口外，背面还具有一个额外的2.5Gbps端口，您可以为WAN或LAN进行配置。 2.5gig WAN端口听起来像将来会变得越来越有用，但是我认为还没有任何消费级调制解调器可以利用这一优势。 Comcast提供2Gbps的服务，但这似乎依赖能够在两个千兆位以太网端口之间进行链路聚合的调制解调器。 Google的2Gbps互联网服务非常有限，但是它带有自己的网络设备，根据注册页面，“客户将无法使用自己的路由器”。我想为未来做计划！ 
这些路由器上的大多数规格都只是重申了标准的Wi-Fi 6 / 6E功能，但华硕确实列出了具有1GB RAM的1.8GHz 64位四核CPU，这听起来可以在不降低速度的情况下处理大量流量。该路由器支持5GHz和6GHz的4x4 MU-MIMO，理论最高速度为4.8Gbps。侧面还有两个USB 3.2端口，用于廉价的NAS设置。
     Netgear Nighthawk AXE11000 Wi-Fi路由器请求进入星际基地的许可。夜鹰似乎达到了与华硕路由器相同的规格，但价格却高出50美元：高达599.99美元。
 如果听起来很熟悉，请阻止我：一个1.8GHz四核处理器和一个内存，用于5GHz和6GHz的4x4 MU-MIMO，两个USB 3.0端口，千兆位WAN，4个千兆位局域网以及一个用于WAN或LAN的2.5gig端口。就像华硕路由器一样，有八个天线，但不是蜘蛛式设计，每个翼尖都装有四个天线。
 Netgear即将参加Wi-Fi 6E路由器竞赛，预计将于3月15日上市。
     如果您想知道白色的Xbox Series X会是什么样子，请与Linksys AXE8400接触。这很容易成为新Wi-Fi 6E路由器中最不疯狂的设计，并且由于将路由器放置在集中的可见位置是您可以为连接而做的最好的事情之一，这对您来说可能是一件大事。 （有趣的事实：Linksys的这种设计至少可以追溯到2018年，因此比新Xbox早了很长时间！如果您曾经想要过Microsoft的微型版本，甚至还有黑色的旧版本。 ＃39;游戏机。）
    Linksys的区别在于它是网状路由器，一包的价格为449.99美元，两包的价格为849.99美元，三包的价格为1,199.99美元。 Linksys的新闻稿将美国发布日期定为＆＃34; 2021年春/夏季。并将在2021年下半年实现全球可用性。＆＃34;网状路由器具有从开放6GHz频谱中受益最快的潜力，因为它们可以将6GHz用于接入点之间的回程，从而完全释放5GHz的空间用于客户端连接。
 目前还没有公开数据表，关于AXE8400的重要细节很少。只是从图片中可以看到，速度不确定的背面有四个LAN端口，以及一个USB 3.x端口。 WAN端口标记为＆＃34; 5Gbps Internet，＆＃34;因此，听起来好像Linksys已真正准备好用于具有数千兆位端口的调制解调器（如果有的话）。 
Linksys现在发布的一个有趣的技术规格是AXE8400具有＆＃34; Qualcomm Networking Pro 1210＆＃34;芯片组是高通公司的指定平台，是希望构建Wi-Fi 6E路由器的公司的指定平台。这是基于14nm处理技术构建的四核2.2Ghz Cortex A53芯片。
 Linksys的该网站还在戏弄看起来像非网状Wi-Fi 6E路由器，但我们甚至还没有名字。
     最后，我们没有发布日期或价格，我们有一对TP-Link路由器。首先是TP-Link Archer AX206，它具有带4×4 MU-MIMO的Wi-Fi 6E。这里有趣的是一些严肃的有线网络选项。令人遗憾的TP-Link只提供了一个俯视图，因为这东西的背面听起来很忙：一个USB-C端口，一个USB-A端口，四个千兆以太网端口，一个2.5 Gbps WAN / LAN端口，10Gbps WAN / LAN端口，以及当您想变得很认真的时候使用的10Gbps SFP + WAN / LAN端口，用于光纤连接。哇
  SFP +端口吸引了人们的注意，但是我不能说我真的了解这里的市场细分。 SFP +端口通常用于企业型网络（或家庭实验室人群）中的回传，用于将网络机架中的一台设备连接到另一台设备。我无法想象一个美国人想用10英尺长的杆触碰这些消费级塑料组合盒之一。我很难想象要向非网络爱好者出售40美元左右的光缆，即使您能做到这一点，TP-Link的消费者部门也不会出售任何其他SFP +设备。在这个我们甚至无法让调制解调器制造商超越千兆以太网的世界中，一个普通人应该怎么用SPF +端口做什么？我猜想更多的选择总会更好，但是我们必须看看这些花哨的，有用的额外功能的价格是多少。最终来自TP-Link的是Archer AX96，虽然同样没有价格或发布日期，但听起来这将是一款更便宜的Wi-Fi 6E路由器。主要提示是6GHz Wi-Fi 6E的速度等级，仅达到＆＃34; 2402 Mbps，＆＃34;这表明该路由器仅在6GHz频谱上执行2x2 MIMO，是华硕和Netgear路由器速度的一半。 5GHz仍然看起来像4x4 MIMO，因此该路由器将允许您迈入6GHz。
 在背面（再次没有图片），有2.5Gbps和1Gbps WAN / LAN端口，三个1Gbps LAN端口，一个USB 3端口和一个USB 2端口。
note_en:
Wi-Fi 6E is very slowly coming to a product near you. The Wi-Fi Alliance started certifying devices on  January 7, and CES 2021 saw plenty of product announcements related to the Wi-Fi 6E rollout.
 Wi-Fi 6E, if you haven't heard, is a new standard for Wi-Fi that was approved by  the FCC last year. While Wi-Fi 6 (no "e," aka 802.11ax) is a  bunch of technical improvements mostly aimed at more efficient usage of existing spectrum, Wi-Fi 6E is all about expanding Wi-Fi to a newly freed-up chunk of spectrum. Previously, Wi-Fi only worked on the 2.4Ghz and 5GHz spectrum, but Wi-Fi 6E uses the 6GHz spectrum. In the United States, 6E has a huge chunk of continuous spectrum—1200MHz. Previously, 5GHz only offered 140MHz of useful, non-DFS spectrum, and 2.4 GHz only had 70MHz of very crowded spectrum, which is vulnerable to running microwaves and other interference.
 Neither Wi-Fi 6 or Wi-Fi 6E is about more speed—both are more about dealing with Wi-Fi capacity issues, which frequently rear their heads in apartment buildings and large public gatherings. If your Wi-Fi is currently terrible due to crowded airwaves in a densely populated area, Wi-Fi 6E could greatly improve your wireless performance. Getting on Wi-Fi 6E will mean buying new clients and new access points, though, hence this roundup article.
  The highest-profile device at CES was the  Samsung Galaxy S21, and while the cheaper variants don't have Wi-Fi 6E, the $1,200 "Ultra" model does, and by  most accounts, it is "the world's first Wi-Fi 6E phone." Expect this trend to continue for most Android phones in 2021. Wi-Fi 6E is an option on the Snapdragon 888 SoC that will end up in most flagship devices, and you'll probably see the more expensive models adopt it.
 Wi-Fi 6E is coming to laptops, too. MSI's  GE76 Raider looks like it will be the first Wi-Fi 6E laptop, thanks to Intel's AX210 Wi-Fi card. The Intel AX210 card is  for sale to users, by the way, so you can upgrade most desktops and laptops to Wi-Fi 6E yourself, right now, with an add-in card. Clients are coming, so what we need are Wi-Fi 6E access points, or "routers" when you're talking about the all-in-one consumer-grade network equipment. Most of the big consumer network brands showed up to CES with a Wi-Fi 6E router to show off.
     We're starting with the router closest to hitting general availability: the  Asus ROG Rapture GT-AXE11000. This was announced all the way back in September as the "World's first Wi-Fi 6E gaming router," but it finally got a release window: January 2021.  Newegg currently has it for preorder for a cool $549.99 with a release date of January 29, but some reports  are saying the router is already shipping. Like with many "Gaming" routers, the GT-AXE1100 has a crazy design that looks like it could flip over and start walking around like a spider at any moment.
    Besides the usual 4x gigabit LAN ports on the back and a gigabit WAN port, there's also an extra 2.5Gbps port on the back, which you can configure for WAN or LAN. A 2.5gig WAN port sounds like something that would be increasingly useful in the future, but I don't think there are any consumer-grade modems that could take advantage of that yet. Comcast has a  2Gbps service, but that seems to rely on modems that can do link aggregation across two gigabit Ethernet ports. Google has a very limited  2Gbps Internet service, but that comes with its own networking gear and according to  the sign-up page, "customers will not be able to use their own router." Plan for the future, I guess!
 Most of the specs on these routers just reiterate the standard Wi-Fi 6/6E features, but Asus does list a 1.8GHz 64-bit Quad-core CPU with 1GB RAM, which sounds like it can handle a lot of traffic without slowing down. The router supports 4x4 MU-MIMO for 5GHz and 6GHz, for a theoretical top speed of 4.8Gbps. There are also two USB 3.2 ports on the side for a cheap NAS setup.
     Requesting permission to dock into starbase, it's the  Netgear Nighthawk AXE11000 Wi-Fi Router. The Nighthawk seems to hit all the same specs as the Asus router but is $50 more: a whopping $599.99.
 Stop me if this sounds familiar: a 1.8GHz quad-core processor and a gig of memory, 4x4 MU-MIMO for 5GHz and 6GHz, two USB 3.0 ports, gigabit WAN, 4 gigabit LAN, and a 2.5gig port for WAN or LAN. Just like the Asus router, there are eight antennas, but instead of the spider design, four antennas live in each wingtip.
 Netgear's entry in the Wi-Fi 6E router contest is up for preorder now, with an estimated availability of March 15.
     If you've ever wondered what a white  Xbox Series X would look like, meet the  Linksys AXE8400. This is easily the least-crazy design of the new Wi-Fi 6E routers, and since putting a router in a centralized, visible location is one of the best things you can do for connectivity, that might be a big deal to you. (Fun fact: this Linksys design dates back  at least to 2018 and therefore predates the new Xbox by a long time! There is even an  older version that comes in black, if you've ever wanted a mini-me version of Microsoft's game console.)
    The Linksys has the distinction of being a mesh router and comes in $449.99 for a 1-pack, $849.99 for a 2-pack, and $1,199.99 for a 3-pack. Linksys'  press release lists the US release date as "Spring/Summer 2021" with "global availability to follow in the second half of 2021." Mesh routers have the potential to benefit the most quickly from the 6GHz spectrum opening up, since they  could use 6GHz for the backhaul between access points, freeing up 5GHz purely for client connections.
 Wth no public datasheet yet, very little important detail is provided about the AXE8400 right now. Just from the pictures, there are four LAN ports on the back of indeterminate speed, along with a single USB 3.x port. The WAN port is labeled "5Gbps Internet," so it sounds like Linksys is really ready for modems with multi-gigabit ports, if they are ever produced.
 The one interesting tech spec Linksys is publishing right now is that the AXE8400 has a " Qualcomm Networking Pro 1210" chipset at its heart, Qualcomm's designated platform for companies looking to build a Wi-Fi 6E router. This is a quad-core 2.2Ghz Cortex A53 chip, built on a 14nm process technology.
 Linksys' website is also teasing what looks like a non-mesh Wi-Fi 6E router, but we don't even have a name for it yet.
     Coming in last with no release date or price at all, we have a pair of TP-Link routers. First up, the  TP-Link Archer AX206, which has Wi-Fi 6E with 4×4 MU-MIMO. What's interesting here are some serious wired networking options. It's a shame TP-Link only provided a single, overhead image, because the back of this thing sounds very busy: a USB-C port, a USB-A port, four gigabit Ethernet ports, a 2.5 Gbps WAN/LAN port, a 10Gbps WAN/LAN port, and, for when you want to get  really serious, a 10Gbps  SFP+ WAN/LAN port, for a fiber-optic connection. Whoa.
  The SFP+ port is an attention grabber, but I can't say I really understand the market segmentation here. SFP+ ports are normally used for backhaul in business-style networks (or the  home-lab crowd) for connecting one device in your network rack to another. I can't imagine a Rackmount-American wanting to touch one of these consumer-grade plastic combo-boxes with a 10-foot pole. I have a hard time imagining selling a ~$40 fiber-optic cable to a non-network-enthusiast, and even if you could do that, TP-Link's consumer division doesn't sell any other SFP+ gear. In a world where we can't even get modem manufacturers to move beyond gigabit Ethernet, what is a normal person supposed to do with an SPF+ port? I guess more options are always better, but we'll have to see what the price tag is like for these fancy, questionably useful extras. Finally from TP-Link is the  Archer AX96, and while, again, there is no price or release date, this sounds like it will be a cheaper Wi-Fi 6E router. The main hint is the speed rating for the 6GHz Wi-Fi 6E, which only hits "2402 Mbps," which indicates that this router only does 2x2 MIMO on the 6GHz spectrum, or half the speed of the Asus and Netgear routers. 5GHz still looks like 4x4 MIMO, so this router would allow for a baby step into 6GHz.
 On the back (again, there are no pictures), there are 2.5Gbps and 1Gbps WAN/LAN ports, three 1Gbps LAN ports, one USB 3 port, and a USB 2 port.
title: Wi-Fi 6E抵达CES 2021
